choices.text: Migrate to Azure Database for MySQL Flexible Server with zone-redundant HA, which automatically replaces the need for read replicas with built-in HA
choices.explanation: Flexible Server HA provides automatic failover for high availability, not read replica routing. HA failover is a different capability from using read replicas for reporting query offloading.

choices.text: Use Azure Database Migration Service with online mode to migrate the primary database, then manually configure the two read replicas after cutover
choices.explanation: DMS online mode migrates the primary database but does not handle the replica configuration. The two on-premises read replicas would need to be re-pointed to the new Azure primary manually.

choices.text: Deploy MySQL on Azure Virtual Machines with Availability Set, replicating the on-premises primary-replica topology exactly
choices.explanation: MySQL on Azure VMs provides full control over replication topology but requires managing MySQL server software, OS patching, backups, and failover logic manually. Azure Database for MySQL PaaS handles these operational tasks.

questions.explanation: Azure Database for MySQL read replica from external MySQL primary is designed exactly for this migration scenario. It maintains the replica chain during migration and automatically promotes replicas to primary at cutover.

questions.question:
A large enterprise is migrating from a hybrid on-premises/public cloud environment (AWS EC2 + on-premises vSphere) to Azure. They have 1,000 EC2 instances (Windows and Linux), 500 VMware VMs, and complex cross-cloud networking using AWS Direct Connect and on-premises MPLS.

Which migration and governance approach consolidates all three environments?

choices.text: Use Azure Migrate for all workloads, deploying the Azure Migrate appliance in AWS (for EC2 assessment) and in vSphere (for VM assessment), then migrate all workloads to Azure using Azure Site Recovery
choices.explanation: Azure Migrate can assess VMware and Hyper-V workloads but not AWS EC2 instances. ASR does not support AWS EC2 as a migration source.

choices.text: Use AWS Migration Hub Refactor Spaces to migrate AWS workloads to Azure, while using Azure Migrate for VMware workloads
choices.explanation: AWS Migration Hub Refactor Spaces is for refactoring/migrating between AWS accounts or AWS services, not for cross-cloud migration to Azure.

choices.text: For AWS EC2: use CloudEndure Migration (now part of AWS Application Migration Service) to migrate to Azure VMs. For VMware: use Azure Site Recovery. For cross-cloud networking: deploy Azure Virtual WAN with CloudConnect for AWS Direct Connect integration
choices.explanation: CloudEndure/AWS Application Migration Service supports continuous replication from AWS EC2 to Azure VMs. Azure Site Recovery handles VMware-to-Azure migration. Azure Virtual WAN CloudConnect provides connectivity to AWS Direct Connect and on-premises MPLS through a unified SD-WAN hub.

choices.text: Re-architect all workloads as containers and deploy to Azure Kubernetes Service, using Azure Arc for unified management across the remaining AWS and on-premises environments
choices.explanation: Re-architecting 1,500 workloads as containers in a migration context is not realistic. Azure Arc manages hybrid Azure resources but cannot migrate AWS EC2 instances to AKS.

questions.explanation: A multi-source migration requires purpose-built tools per source: CloudEndure for AWS EC2, ASR for VMware, Virtual WAN CloudConnect for unified network consolidation.

questions.question:
A fintech company is migrating from Google Cloud Platform (GCP) to Azure. They have 300 GKE (Google Kubernetes Engine) pods running microservices with Istio service mesh. The microservices use gRPC for inter-service communication.

Which Azure service mapping preserves the gRPC inter-service communication?

choices.text: Migrate GKE pods to Azure Kubernetes Service with Istio installed, and configure gRPC to use Istio's built-in xDS API for service discovery
choices.explanation: AKS supports Istio natively, and Istio's xDS API (used by Envoy proxies) provides service discovery for gRPC services. gRPC uses HTTP/2 and the xDS protocol for load balancing and service discovery. Istio on AKS provides the same xDS API as Istio on GKE, enabling transparent gRPC communication without code changes.

choices.text: Migrate GKE pods to Azure Container Instances with Azure Service Fabric mesh for gRPC routing
choices.explanation: Azure Container Instances does not support multi-container pod orchestration with service mesh. Service Fabric Mesh is a separate serverless containers platform, not a direct AKS equivalent.

choices.text: Migrate gRPC microservices to Azure Functions with gRPC triggers, replacing the Kubernetes service mesh with Azure API Management for routing
choices.explanation: Azure Functions does not natively support gRPC triggers. Azure Functions uses an in-process runtime that cannot host arbitrary gRPC servers.

choices.text: Use Azure Arc for Kubernetes to manage the GKE cluster from Azure, avoiding migration of the cluster itself
choices.explanation: Azure Arc for Kubernetes allows Azure management of Kubernetes clusters anywhere, but it does not migrate workloads. The GKE cluster remains in GCP.

questions.explanation: Istio is the common service mesh layer across GKE and AKS. The xDS API for gRPC service discovery is compatible between Istio on GKE and Istio on AKS. gRPC applications can migrate with the same service mesh configuration and zero code changes.

questions.question:
An enterprise is migrating from an on-premises Zookeeper/Kafka cluster (50 brokers, 500 topics, 10 million messages/second) to Azure Event Hubs. They use Kafka API for producer/consumer applications. The migration must happen with no application code changes.

Which Event Hubs tier and configuration supports the 10M messages/second throughput?

choices.text: Deploy Azure Event Hubs Standard tier with 20 throughput units, partitioning the 500 topics across Event Hubs namespaces
choices.explanation: Event Hubs Standard has a limit of 1 MB/s ingress per TU. At 10 million messages/second (assuming average 1KB per message = ~10 GB/s ingress), this far exceeds Standard tier capacity even with maximum TU (40 TUs = 40 MB/s).

choices.text: Deploy Azure Event Hubs Dedicated tier with 100 capacity units (CU), providing up to 2 GB/s ingress and 4 GB/s egress, using Kafka Connect to bridge the on-premises Kafka cluster to Event Hubs
choices.explanation: Event Hubs Dedicated provides massive throughput. However, Kafka Connect is used to migrate between two Kafka clusters, not to replace Kafka with Event Hubs. The correct approach is to use the Kafka protocol support in Event Hubs directly.

choices.text: Deploy Azure Event Hubs Dedicated tier with 100 capacity units, and configure producer/consumer applications to use Event Hubs' Kafka-compatible endpoint (bootstrap server URL: <namespace>.servicebus.windows.net:9093)
choices.explanation: Event Hubs provides a Kafka-compatible endpoint on port 9093. Producer/consumer applications configured for Kafka can point to Event Hubs' Kafka endpoint without any code changes (just a bootstrap server URL update). Dedicated tier with 100 CUs provides the 2 GB/s ingress needed for 10M messages/second.

choices.text: Use Apache Kafka on Azure HDInsight (Kafka cluster) to maintain the same Kafka architecture without migrating to Event Hubs
choices.explanation: HDInsight Kafka is a managed Kafka service, not Event Hubs. While this preserves the Kafka API, it does not achieve the migration goal to Event Hubs.

questions.explanation: Event Hubs Dedicated with Kafka protocol compatibility is the direct replacement for on-premises Kafka at scale. The Kafka endpoint (9093) accepts Kafka protocol clients without code changes.

questions.question:
A retail company is migrating from on-premises Cloudflare for DNS and CDN to Azure. They have 50 domain names, a globally distributed CDN with 100 edge locations, and DDoS protection via Cloudflare's L3/L4/L7 protection. The company uses Cloudflare Workers (serverless edge functions) for A/B testing and personalization.

Which Azure service combination replaces the full Cloudflare stack?

choices.text: Use Azure DNS for authoritative DNS, Azure Front Door for CDN and L7 DDoS protection, Azure DDoS Protection Standard for L3/L4 protection, and Azure Functions with Azure CDN for edge compute replacement of Cloudflare Workers
choices.explanation: Azure DNS replaces Cloudflare DNS. Azure Front Door provides CDN + WAF + L7 DDoS protection at global scale. Azure DDoS Protection Standard provides L3/L4 DDoS protection. Azure Functions with geolocation routing can replace Cloudflare Workers for A/B testing and personalization (routing based on headers/cookies). Azure Front Door's rules engine provides URL rewriting and redirect capabilities equivalent to Cloudflare Workers routing logic.

choices.text: Use Azure DNS for DNS, Azure CDN Premium from Edgio for CDN, and Azure Web Application Firewall for DDoS protection, replacing Cloudflare Workers with Azure Functions at the CDN edge
choices.explanation: Azure CDN Premium from Edgio does support edge compute via the Rules Engine, but it is a legacy CDN product. Azure Front Door is the modern replacement for global CDN + security. Azure Functions cannot be deployed at CDN edge locations in the same way Cloudflare Workers runs on Cloudflare's edge.

choices.text: Use Azure Traffic Manager for DNS routing, Azure CDN Standard for Microsoft for static content, and Azure Application Gateway for dynamic content
choices.explanation: Traffic Manager is a DNS-based load balancer, not a CDN. Azure CDN Standard Microsoft is not a true CDN with global edge POPs. Application Gateway is a regional ADC, not a global CDN.

choices.text: Deploy Cloudflare in 'Reverse Proxy' mode through Azure, maintaining Cloudflare as the CDN and security layer while using Azure for backend hosting
choices.explanation: This approach does not migrate away from Cloudflare. The question asks for migration FROM Cloudflare to Azure.

questions.explanation: Azure Front Door + Azure DNS + Azure DDoS Protection + Azure Functions (via Front Door rules engine) provides the full Cloudflare stack replacement: global CDN, DNS, L3-L7 DDoS protection, and edge compute.

questions.question:
A healthcare company is performing a cutover migration of their electronic health record (EHR) system from on-premises to Azure. The system consists of 8 VMs: 2 web servers, 2 application servers, 2 database servers (SQL Server Always On AG), and 2 file servers. The cutover window is 4 hours. They need to validate that the Azure deployment meets the same SLA as the on-premises deployment (99.95% availability).

Which cutover and validation approach should they follow?

choices.text: Perform a blue/green deployment with Azure ASR, deploying the green environment in Azure, running a parallel validation for 24 hours, then switching DNS to Azure with immediate on-premises decommissioning
choices.explanation: A 24-hour parallel validation exceeds the 4-hour cutover window. ASR blue/green is designed for DR scenarios with longer validation periods, not for a planned migration cutover with a strict time window.

choices.text: Use Azure Site Recovery with a planned failover during the 4-hour window, validate the Azure deployment using Azure Monitor Application Insights synthetic transactions, then re-point the load balancer to Azure if all tests pass
choices.explanation: ASR planned failover ensures all VMs failover in a coordinated sequence, preserving the Always On AG configuration. Application Insights availability tests (synthetic transactions) can be pre-configured to run against the Azure deployment during the validation phase. The load balancer switch completes the cutover atomically.

choices.text: Use Azure Migrate to perform a physical cutover, manually start each VM in Azure, run SQL Server Always On AG validation scripts, then update the application connection strings
choices.explanation: Azure Migrate does not perform cutover. It is an assessment and replication orchestration tool. Manual VM startup and AG validation scripts do not constitute a coordinated cutover process.

choices.text: Deploy Azure Landing Zone with all VMs pre-provisioned, use Azure Bastion for remote access during validation, run SQL Server DBCC CHECKDB for data integrity, then perform a DNS cutover with a 5-minute TTL
choices.explanation: Pre-provisioning VMs without ASR replication means no incremental data sync - the last sync point could be hours/days old. 5-minute TTL does not account for client-side DNS caching.

questions.explanation: ASR planned failover ensures coordinated VM failover, preserving Always On AG. Application Insights synthetic transactions validate the application stack end-to-end. The load balancer switch completes the cutover atomically within the 4-hour window.

questions.question:
After completing a large-scale migration to Azure, the security team needs to validate that all Azure resources comply with the company's security baseline. The baseline includes: all storage accounts must use HTTPS, no public IP addresses on VMs, all SQL databases must have Defender for Cloud enabled, and all secrets must be in Key Vault.

Which Azure governance tool provides the MOST efficient automated compliance validation?

choices.text: Use Azure Security Center (Microsoft Defender for Cloud) with the 'Regulatory Compliance' dashboard configured with the company security baseline as a custom compliance package, enabling continuous assessment
choices.explanation: Microsoft Defender for Cloud with the Regulatory Compliance dashboard provides continuous assessment against compliance frameworks. The company can import their security baseline as a custom initiative (JSON policy set) and Defender for Cloud will continuously assess all subscriptions against it, providing a compliance score and specific resource-level non-compliance alerts.

choices.text: Use Azure Policy with audit effect for each security requirement, and run Get-AzPolicyCompliance PowerShell commands manually on a weekly schedule
choices.explanation: Azure Policy with audit effect provides compliance state, but manual PowerShell execution on a weekly schedule is not continuous assessment. Defender for Cloud provides the same policy evaluation but with continuous monitoring, alerting, and integration with security workflows.

choices.text: Use Azure Advisor to identify security recommendations and export them to a CSV for the security team to review
choices.explanation: Azure Advisor provides best-practice recommendations, not a formal compliance validation framework. It does not evaluate against a custom security baseline.

choices.text: Use Azure Sentinel to query security events and generate a weekly compliance report using KQL queries
choices.explanation: Azure Sentinel is a SIEM for security event monitoring, not a compliance assessment tool. KQL queries against SecurityEvent table only covers Windows security events, not Azure resource configuration compliance.

questions.explanation: Microsoft Defender for Cloud's continuous compliance assessment against custom security initiatives is the most efficient post-migration validation approach. It automatically discovers all resources across subscriptions and evaluates them against the custom baseline.

questions.question:
A company has completed the data migration phase of an Oracle-to-Azure SQL Managed Instance project. The data migration used Oracle Data Guard for continuous replication during the migration window. The final cutover is planned for next weekend. The team wants to validate data integrity before cutover.

Which validation approach should they use?

choices.text: Use Oracle DBMS_COMPARISON package to compare tables between Oracle and Azure SQL Managed Instance
choices.explanation: DBMS_COMPARISON is an Oracle-specific package that cannot compare across different database engines (Oracle vs Azure SQL). It only compares two Oracle databases.

choices.text: Use Azure Data Factory with a data flow that performs row-count validation and checksum comparison (MD5/SHA) between Oracle source and Azure SQL Managed Instance target, generating a validation report
choices.explanation: ADF Data Flows can connect to both Oracle (via integration runtime) and Azure SQL MI (via managed identity), performing row-count and column-level checksum comparisons. MD5 or SHA256 hashes computed in Data Flow expressions enable byte-level data integrity validation across heterogeneous databases.

choices.text: Use Oracle GoldenGate's Integrated Extract to pull data from Oracle and use the Integrated Replicat to apply to Azure SQL Managed Instance
choices.explanation: GoldenGate Integrated Replicat cannot apply to Azure SQL Managed Instance. Azure SQL Managed Instance is not a supported GoldenGate target.

choices.text: Export Oracle data to CSV files, upload to Azure Blob Storage, then use Azure SQL Migration extension for VS Code to validate the CSV data against the Azure SQL Managed Instance data
choices.explanation: CSV export/import introduces manual steps and data freshness issues. The Azure SQL Migration extension is a migration assessment tool, not a cross-database data validation tool.

questions.explanation: ADF Data Flow with hash-based comparison is the recommended approach for cross-engine data validation. The checksum approach (MD5/SHA256 of key columns) validates byte-level data integrity across Oracle and Azure SQL MI.

questions.question:
A global logistics company has completed a migration of 200 microservices from on-premises Kubernetes to Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS). The microservices use Helm 3 for packaging and deployment. The team wants to establish a GitOps-based CI/CD pipeline in Azure that validates and deploys to AKS with minimal manual intervention.

Which Azure-native GitOps CI/CD approach should they implement?

choices.text: Use Azure DevOps Pipelines with Helm deploy tasks, triggering on git commits to the repository, deploying directly to AKS using the Kubernetes service connection
choices.explanation: Azure DevOps Pipelines with direct kubectl/Helm deployment is a traditional CI/CD approach, not GitOps. GitOps requires that the cluster state is always derived from git, not from a pipeline.

choices.text: Use Azure Arc for Kubernetes with Azure Policy to enforce cluster configuration from git, combined with Azure DevOps for application CI builds that push images to Azure Container Registry
choices.explanation: Azure Arc for Kubernetes enables GitOps through Azure Policy-based cluster configuration management. Flux or GitOps operator is deployed on the AKS cluster via Arc, pulling Kubernetes manifests from the git repository. Azure DevOps handles application CI (build/push images to ACR), while Arc+Flux handles deployment CD (pull from git + apply to cluster). This is the Azure-native GitOps pattern.

choices.text: Use Azure Kubernetes Service with Azure Policy add-on (built-in) for GitOps, where the cluster automatically syncs from an Azure Container Registry config repository
choices.explanation: AKS Azure Policy add-on evaluates resource requests against cluster-level policies but does not provide GitOps functionality. GitOps in AKS is provided by Azure Arc for Kubernetes, not by the AKS Policy add-on.

choices.text: Deploy Jenkins on AKS for CI/CD, using the Jenkins Kubernetes plugin to deploy Helm charts from a git repository to AKS
choices.explanation: Running Jenkins on AKS is a valid approach but not Azure-native. Jenkins on AKS still implements pipeline-based deployment, not GitOps.

questions.explanation: Azure Arc for Kubernetes + Azure DevOps + ACR is the Azure-native GitOps stack: DevOps for CI (build/push images), Arc + Flux for CD (git-ops deployment to AKS). This separates concerns between application CI and cluster deployment CD while maintaining git as the source of truth.

questions.question:
A company has migrated their entire on-premises SharePoint Server 2019 farm (3 servers: 2 WFE, 1 App) to SharePoint Online in Microsoft 365. They have 15 custom SharePoint solutions (farm solutions .wsp files) and 10 SharePoint Designer workflows. After migration, users report that the custom solutions and workflows are not functioning.

Which post-migration remediation approach restores functionality with the LEAST effort?

choices.text: Re-deploy all 15 .wsp farm solutions to SharePoint Online using the SharePoint Management Shell
choices.explanation: SharePoint Online does not support farm solutions (.wsp files). Farm solutions are a SharePoint Server-specific deployment mechanism that requires farm-level trust and server-side execution.

choices.text: Re-implement all 15 .wsp farm solutions as SharePoint Framework (SPFx) web parts and convert all SharePoint Designer workflows to Power Automate flows
choices.explanation: Re-implementing 15 farm solutions as SPFx and converting 10 workflows to Power Automate is a re-development effort, not a migration remediation. This is a significant effort that can take months.

choices.text: Implement a hybrid SharePoint environment: configure SharePoint Server on Azure VMs for farm solutions and SharePoint Designer workflows, connected to SharePoint Online via Hybrid Workload
choices.explanation: SharePoint Server on Azure VMs supports farm solutions and SharePoint Designer workflows. Azure VMs with SharePoint Server 2019 can be configured as a Hybrid environment with SharePoint Online using OAuth/SAML federation. Hybrid Workloads enable a gradual transition: custom solutions stay on Azure VMs, SharePoint Online handles modern workloads. This preserves functionality while providing a path to full modernization.

choices.text: Use the SharePoint Migration Tool (SPMT) with the '-HybridFarm' flag to re-migrate the farm solutions to SharePoint Online with compatibility layer enabled
choices.explanation: SPMT does not have a '-HybridFarm' flag. SPMT cannot migrate farm solutions - those are server-side code, not content.

questions.explanation: SharePoint Online cannot run farm solutions (.wsp) or SharePoint Designer workflows. The only supported path is: (1) keep them on SharePoint Server 2019 on Azure VMs in a hybrid configuration, or (2) re-develop as SPFx/Power Automate. Hybrid is the least-effort path that restores functionality immediately.

questions.question:
A large healthcare organization is migrating from a legacy on-premises Epic EHR system to Azure. The Epic system uses a relational database (Cache), HL7 FHIR APIs for integrations, and runs on a 3-tier architecture. The migration must maintain HIPAA compliance and support 99.99% availability. The cutover window is limited to 4 hours.

Which migration approach is the only realistic path that preserves HIPAA compliance and the 4-hour cutover?

choices.text: Use Azure Virtual Machines with the same 3-tier architecture, migrating the Cache database using the Epic-provided migration toolkit, and re-implementing FHIR APIs as Azure API Management frontends
choices.explanation: Epic does not provide a migration toolkit for Azure. Epic systems are highly customized and require Epic's own implementation team for any migration. Azure VMs with Epic are not a supported configuration.

choices.text: Use Azure API Management to front the existing on-premises Epic system as a hybrid solution, gradually migrating integration endpoints to Azure PaaS while maintaining the on-premises Epic database
choices.explanation: This is a hybrid approach that does not migrate the EHR system to Azure. Epic on-premises remains the system of record. This is an integration architecture, not a migration.

choices.text: Migrate to Epic on Azure through Epic's own Cloud Platform program, where Epic hosts and manages the Epic environment in their own Azure subscription under a BAA, and the customer migrates to Epic's cloud-hosted solution
choices.explanation: Epic offers a Cloud Platform program where Epic hosts Epic systems in Azure under Epic's own Azure subscription. This is the ONLY supported path for moving Epic to Azure. Epic does not allow customers to self-host Epic in their own Azure subscription. The customer migrates data to Epic's cloud-hosted environment using Epic's data migration tools, with Epic managing the Azure infrastructure under their BAA.

choices.text: Re-host the Epic EHR on Azure Virtual Machines using Epic's supported virtualization platform, implementing Azure Site Recovery for the cutover
choices.explanation: Epic does not certify Azure VMs as a deployment platform for production Epic EHR systems. Epic only certifies specific on-premises and some cloud environments (Epic on Azure is available but only through Epic's own cloud hosting agreements, not through customer-managed Azure subscriptions).

questions.explanation: Epic cannot be migrated to Azure by the customer. Epic's Cloud Platform program is the ONLY supported path - Epic hosts the system in their own Azure subscription with their own BAA. This is fundamentally different from typical application migrations.

questions.question:
An enterprise is migrating 2,000 on-premises Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 VMs to Azure. Due to Red Hat's subscription model, they want to minimize new Red Hat subscriptions by using Azure's Red Hat Gold Image. They currently have 1,500 active Red Hat subscriptions and 500 expired subscriptions.

Which Azure licensing approach should they recommend?

choices.text: Use Azure Red Hat Gold Images for all 2,000 VMs, which include Red Hat Enterprise Linux licenses bundled with Azure infrastructure costs, eliminating the need to manage subscriptions
choices.explanation: Azure Red Hat Gold Images include Red Hat Enterprise Linux licenses billed through Azure. Azure provides Red Hat Gold Images that bundle RHEL with the VM cost, eliminating the need for separate Red Hat subscriptions. For 500 expired subscriptions, this is the most cost-effective approach. For the 1,500 active subscriptions, Azure Hybrid Benefit can be used to bring those licenses to Azure, reducing Azure costs by using existing subscriptions.

choices.text: Use Azure Red Hat Gold Images for the 500 expired subscription VMs, and use Red Hat Cloud Access to transfer the 1,500 existing subscriptions to Azure for the remaining VMs
choices.explanation: Red Hat Cloud Access allows transferring existing Red Hat subscriptions to Azure, but it requires the customer to maintain the subscriptions in Red Hat's subscription management system. Azure Red Hat Gold Images are simpler and the cost is bundled.

choices.text: Use Azure Red Hat Gold Images for all VMs and purchase new Red Hat subscriptions for all 2,000 VMs
choices.explanation: Azure Red Hat Gold Images include RHEL licenses - purchasing additional subscriptions would be double-licensing and unnecessary.

choices.text: Use Azure Red Hat Gold Images with Red Hat Identity Management integration for centralized subscription tracking
choices.explanation: Red Hat Identity Management is for Linux identity management (SSSD, LDAP, Kerberos), not for subscription tracking.

questions.explanation: Azure Red Hat Gold Images bundle RHEL licensing with Azure VM costs. For the 1,500 active subscriptions, Azure Hybrid Benefit can offset costs. For the 500 expired subscriptions, Azure Red Hat Gold Images provide compliant RHEL without needing to renew expired subscriptions.

questions.question:
A media company is migrating 500 TB of video assets from on-premises HDFS to Azure. They currently use Apache Spark with Delta Lake for data processing. The Spark jobs read and write Parquet files to HDFS. After migration to ADLS Gen2, they want to continue using Delta Lake.

Which configuration change allows Delta Lake to work with ADLS Gen2 without modifying Spark job code?

choices.text: Configure Spark to use the Azure Blob File System (ABFS) driver by setting spark.hadoop.fs.defaultFS to abfs://<container>@<storage>.dfs.core.windows.net/
choices.explanation: Delta Lake on Azure uses the ABFS (Azure Blob File System) driver, which is the Azure-native Hadoop-compatible filesystem driver for ADLS Gen2. Setting spark.hadoop.fs.defaultFS to the abfs:// endpoint allows Spark jobs to read/write Delta Lake tables on ADLS Gen2 without any code changes. Delta Lake automatically stores transaction logs and table data as Parquet files in the specified path.

choices.text: Configure Spark to use wasb:// (Windows Azure Storage Blob) driver to maintain compatibility with the on-premises HDFS configuration
choices.explanation: wasb:// is the legacy Azure Blob Storage driver for Hadoop, not ADLS Gen2. wasb:// does not support the hierarchical namespace required for Delta Lake transaction log semantics.

choices.text: Mount ADLS Gen2 as an HDFS filesystem using Azure Data Lake Store connector for Spark, maintaining the hdfs:// protocol in Spark job code
choices.explanation: ADLS Gen2 cannot be mounted as HDFS. The HDFS connector for ADLS Gen2 is the ABFS driver itself, not a separate mount.

choices.text: Replace Delta Lake with Apache Iceberg on ADLS Gen2, as Iceberg has better ADLS Gen2 compatibility
choices.explanation: Delta Lake fully supports ADLS Gen2 via the ABFS driver. Replacing Delta Lake with Iceberg is a re-engineering decision, not a configuration change.

questions.explanation: Delta Lake on Azure uses ABFS (Azure Blob File System) driver, which is the Hadoop-compatible filesystem driver for ADLS Gen2. Setting the Spark defaultFS to abfs:// enables Delta Lake to work on ADLS Gen2 without code changes.

questions.question:
A global manufacturing company is consolidating IT environments from 3 recent acquisitions into Azure. Each acquired company ran a mix of workloads: Company A ran exclusively on AWS (EC2, RDS, S3), Company B ran on Google Cloud (GKE, Cloud SQL, Cloud Storage), and Company C ran on-premises VMware. The consolidation must be completed within 6 months.

Which Azure service combination is the ONLY approach that can migrate all three environments in the 6-month timeframe?

choices.text: Use Azure Migrate for all three environments: assess VMware for Company C, assess AWS EC2 for Company A using the Azure Migrate appliance, and assess GKE for Company B using Azure Arc
choices.explanation: Azure Migrate does not support AWS EC2 assessment. Azure Arc does not assess GKE clusters. There is no single Azure tool that can assess all three environments.

choices.text: For Company A (AWS): use CloudEndure Migration. For Company B (GCP): use Google Migrate for Anthos. For Company C (VMware): use Azure Site Recovery. Parallel workstreams across all three teams enable 6-month completion
choices.explanation: Each cloud environment requires its own native migration tool: CloudEndure/AWS Application Migration Service for AWS EC2 (the standard AWS-to-Azure migration tool), Google Migrate for Anthos or GKE migration tools for GCP GKE (Google provides migration tooling for GKE to GKE on Azure via GKE on Azure or container migration paths), and Azure Site Recovery for VMware. Parallel workstreams across the three acquisitions with dedicated teams for each environment can achieve the 6-month timeline.

choices.text: Containerize all workloads from all three companies and deploy to Azure Kubernetes Service as the unified platform, using Azure Arc for hybrid management across all environments during migration
choices.explanation: Containerizing all workloads from three different cloud environments (AWS EC2, GCP GKE, VMware) within 6 months is a re-engineering effort, not a migration. This approach would not complete within 6 months.

choices.text: Use Azure Data Factory to extract data from all three source environments, transform in Azure, and land in Azure data services, replacing all source applications with Azure-native alternatives
choices.explanation: ADF is a data integration tool, not an application migration tool. It cannot migrate running applications from AWS EC2, GCP GKE, or VMware. This approach would require re-building all applications from scratch.

questions.explanation: Multi-cloud/multi-platform migrations require purpose-built tools per source environment. CloudEndure for AWS, GCP migration tooling for GKE, and ASR for VMware are the only realistic tools for each environment. No single Azure tool covers all three.
